data.title: Public Storage's Slow Phase Is A Long-Term Opportunity
data.description:
Public Storage (PSA) is going through a transition where near-term organic growth remains soft, but long-term fundamentals and platform advantages are still intact. While margins are still high, the balance sheet is still strong, and dividends are well covered, there are some concerns around this phase of expansion through capital allocation (rather than rent health).

Current valuations sit near the lower end of PSA’s historical range, reflecting muted growth expectations. That opens up an opportunity for long-term investors to gain from the eventual normalization of margins in new properties and a supply tightness at the end of this phase that could boost organic rent growth.

What Supports PSA

PSA's same-store NOI margins are strong and steady. At 78.5% reported in Q3 2025, gross margins indicate that storage remains a high-margin business and that is holding over the past year (margins were 78.4% in Q3 2024). That enables PSA to protect profitability and cushions it in weak demand periods, reducing risks of earnings falling sharply.

Payroll and marketing costs are actually falling. The same store expenses data shows onsite payroll expenses down 6.1% and marketing expenses are lower by 4.1%. This shows efficiencies in progress and operational levers that can be extended to even newly acquired properties in the long run. For now, the operating cost leverage becomes even more important because PSA is potentially going through a phase of lower initial margins in newly acquired properties. Other operating costs, notably higher property taxes, are offsetting most of these gains. Property taxes have increased by 4.5% YTD and are expected to be as high as 5.5% for the full year across the same store portfolio.

The third supportive factor for PSA is that realized rent per occupied square foot is still holding up well even in a soft period—up 0.6% YoY. Existing customers rate increases are still sticking. There may be weaker pricing power in the case of new tenants, but existing customers are still paying higher over time and cushioning overall rental income.

Earnings Impact Gradual

PSA's growth engine looks modest overall and is supported primarily by external expansion and leasing activity rather than organic same-store growth. The development pipeline of 3.9m square feet and a remaining spend of ~381m over the next 18-24 months appears controlled but meaningful. However, this is not booming growth we are talking about.

The non-same store NOI growth is up by ~$47m while same store NOI is down slightly, indicating inorganic growth. This is therefore a growth era banking on capital deployment and execution rather than any structural cycle-driven rent expansion fundamentals. PSA is deploying ~$1.3b in acquisitions and ~$650m in the development pipeline. That exposes the growth prospects to demand and execution risks—not reliable growth from already stabilized assets. That also implies the earnings impact will be gradual as execution risks and initial lower margins weigh.

At the same time, organic fundamentals are not going through an expansion phase either. Same-store occupancy is drifting lower. Average occupancy is down 0.5% YoY (end-period occupancy down 0.7% YoY). This is not a material worry at this point but shows demand is not really improving meaningfully. Importantly, with supply not in shortage, a pricing recovery is unlikely to happen soon.

Balance Sheet Reasonably Controlled

Since PSA is now in a capital deployment phase, the balance sheet becomes an important area to monitor too. Over the last two and a half years, real estate assets have increased by ~$5.2b (up 20-25%), as is expected given the uptick in acquisition and development activity. A large part of this growth is funded by the balance sheet leverage. During the same period, net debt has grown by ~$3.5b. However, the earnings uplift is gradual while the asset base is rising fast—gradual because of initially lower occupancy and higher operating costs. This implies PSA will go through a period of elevated debt to EBITDA ratio. The current debt to EBITDA ratio is ~2.9x, which is reasonable by REIT standards, although it is expectedly higher than the 2-2.5x range we have seen before.

Overall, PSA still has a strong balance sheet but has had to increase leverage (rather than more retained cash flow) to fund expansion. The retained earnings have dropped from negative ~$160m to negative ~$1.1b from March 2023 to now, clearly showing how retained earnings are weaker after accounting for dividends and depreciation. At the equity level too, common equity has not grown in line with the asset base. Liquidity levels have also fluctuated through this period with cash balances moving up and down between quarters—implying dynamic capital deployment over idle liquidity during this asset expansion phase. None of these changes in PSA's capital structure are at levels yet that raise caution but need to be monitored, especially until earnings growth from new acquisitions starts to show up.

Dividends and Coverage

With an annualized core FFO of ~$17 and an annual dividend outflow of $12 ($3 monthly), the payout ratio is ~70% and retains a healthy cushion for the over 4% yields in the near term. However, yield alone does not clinch the thesis. PSA's price fluctuations are material, and 20-30% moves within a few months are not rare. That is, within a year, the price move alone is capable of wiping out 5-8 years of yields. The yield is stable, but valuations are a bigger determinant of fresh entry timing for PSA. Yields are an add-on to that Buy thesis on valuations.

Valuations Support Entry

Barring the sector-wide repricing effect in 2022-23, the price-to-FFO ratio is near the lowest healthy point seen in the past decade. PSA demands a premium relative to the sector median because of its conservative balance sheet and high margins. The platform play, wherein PSA has a proven ability to buy under-managed assets and lift margins, also contributes to that premium.

A lot of those factors that generally support PSA's valuations are still intact. The balance sheet is becoming only marginally more leveraged but still remains sufficiently below most REIT peers' leverage levels. The recent drop in valuations relative to history is potentially a reflection of markets factoring in weak same-store pricing power and a regime of growth mostly enabled by capital deployment rather than rent acceleration tailwinds.

That valuation compression is an opportunity for long-term investors with patient capital and the ability to ride volatility (even top-up positions if prices correct without any sharp change in fundamentals). While margins from new properties will only have a gradual effect on earnings in the short term, overall FFO can compound at 4-5% in the long term, both due to maturing operations in new assets and same-store improvements once supply slows. Such a regime could also open up scope for rerating in addition to FFO expansion itself. That is, fresh buys today can expect 4-5% from dividends and 4-5% from FFO expansion annually and over the next 2-3 years. That is high single-digit returns without including the impact of the valuation rerating optionality.

Overall, PSA is a decent Buy at current levels. But I will watch a few confirmation signals over the next few quarters to see whether the turn I expect is in progress. Starting with Q4 results, investors should see a narrowing of declines in move-in rent trends to -5% levels (and eventually flat at some point, if not immediately). In Q4, even minor improvement from the 10% declines reported in Q3 should count. Occupancy trends should also stabilize, i.e., stop drifting down. It is not a material drift as of now, but further drift should be arrested to retain the turnaround narrative. Leverage stabilization and non-same-store margin lifts will also be in focus.

I do not expect organic growth to return over the next few quarters till supply tightens. Somewhere around the end of the year, same-store revenue growth turning positive will be another key metric I will expect to show up. This Buy call in PSA may require multiple entries over the year if valuations compress further and depending on how the next few quarterly reports show margin improvements. PSA may not offer immediate upside, but it looks like a steady compounder—paying you to wait while gradual growth and a better industry backdrop slowly do their work.

data.title: Luckin Coffee: A Once-In-A-Decade Growth Machine
data.description:
I have already analyzed Dutch Bros (BROS) (check it here) and Black Rock Coffee Bar (BRCB) (check it here), which, in my opinion, are very interesting stories that show that the classic industry that was practically dominated by Starbucks (SBUX) still has very compelling prospects that allow for the construction of a business model capable of scaling up significantly.

However, these two do not compete with SBUX in size, although they are still positive stories about this coffee-focused QSR business model. The company that can really do this and even has real potential to eventually dethrone (not only in size but also as a benchmark, shareholder value, and even revenue, eventually) Starbucks is Luckin Coffee (LKNCY).

And the main thing is that, in addition to having this potential to surpass Starbucks, LKNCY, on this path, should generate a lot of shareholder value. Therefore, my rating is a buy.

I’ve Never Seen Anything Like That

I have covered many retail companies, and even so, I can't think of any example of a listed company that has shown the pace of expansion that Luckin Coffee is showing. It's something that even makes you stop and think, “Did I understand these numbers correctly? Are they reporting something wrong?” and that’s why the chart below is very appealing; they opened almost 8 thousand stores in one year.

In the last quarter alone, 3,000 stores were opened. Do you know any other company that managed to deliver this kind of growth? I don't, so if you do, feel free to share this info in the comments section.

But also, 99% of these Luckin stores are located in China. I mean, this is the main reason for the attractive valuation; there are many more uncertainties when comparing to the global SBUX profile.

Also, I know we are talking about a huge country, but Luckin Coffee has already reached more than 112 million transacting customers, with growth of tens of millions YoY. This is impressive even for China; management deserves the merit.

For instance, if we take 112 million customers, we are talking about practically ~8% of China's population; there is still a lot of space available for growth.

When I see this kind of pace of expansion, one of the things I usually think is, "They must be sacrificing margin". Well, sort of. But at least I expected worse numbers. The store-level operating margin went from ~23.5% to 17.5% (YoY comparison). It is a big difference, but it's acceptable when you remember that Luckin is still maturing ~3,000 stores that opened in the last quarter. Also, there were interesting gains in operating leverage (see the image below).

I think the subtitle of this section sums it up well. I've never seen any company growing like Luckin (have you?).

Luckin Coffee is Already Better Than SBUX

It may seem unfair to compare Starbucks with Luckin Coffee initially, especially because of SBUX’s momentum right now. While the Chinese one is growing rapidly, the other is at a more mature stage, trying to get its house in order and optimize its margins and growth. But the truth is that, in several respects, Luckin Coffee is already doing (much) better than Starbucks.

In terms of number of stores, Starbucks has about 22,600, and as already mentioned, Luckin Coffee recently surpassed 29,000. But what impresses me most is that Starbucks' global comparable sales grew 1% in fiscal Q4, while Luckin Coffee's grew 14% YoY (in RMB). Again, there are still many Luckin Coffee stores in a maturing phase, with recent initiatives emerging and maturing, but there is a very large gap in this number. And this is not a geographical problem either, since if we consider China alone, Starbucks grew comp sales by 2% YoY.

But still, if you look at market cap, Luckin Coffee has a much smaller market cap than Starbucks. While Luckin Coffee has a market cap of $10.7 billion, Starbucks is worth over $100 billion. One explanation is that Luckin Coffee's revenue is ~$2 billion per quarter, while Starbucks is still generating almost $10 billion per quarter (albeit with a compressed margin, so the operating income of the two is similar). And remember, Luckin has more stores than Starbucks, so the explanation also lies in the business model, as Luckin has a greater focus on convenience, value, and delivery, while Starbucks is still a more “premium” store, also focused on experience and the like.

But even if we were to consider a better margin than Starbucks is delivering now, higher revenue does not explain the whole story of LKNCY's market cap. The other part of the explanation lies in the multiples and the value attributed by the market.

Look at these two tables below. While Luckin is trading at a P/E of 14x based on 2026 earnings, Starbucks is trading at 33x, more than double. In other words, for Luckin to surpass Starbucks in market cap in these terms, it would have to have a net income twice that of Starbucks.

So if the market continues to assign this premium to Starbucks, it will be difficult for Luckin to surpass it. But in other areas, it seems quite obvious to me that Luckin can be better than Starbucks.

The main thing is shareholder value. In the earnings call, it was mentioned that the company remains ambitious about expansion, maintaining an aggressive pace in China in 2026, and looking at the international market, such as the US, more cautiously. And in the long term, the ambition is to become a “world-class brand”:

“Together, we will continue building a world-class coffee brand and making Luckin a part of everyone's daily life. As we move forward, we remain committed to long-term value creation for our customers, partners and shareholders.”

This gives me a little more confidence in a model of rapid but healthy expansion. But as it is still difficult to execute, I will consider more conservative assumptions, for example, opening 4,000 units per year until 2030, reaching 50,000 (an ambitious goal, but considering the store model, China + International can easily accommodate it). As for revenue per store, I will consider a growth of 4% per year, based on Q3 2025, reflecting also some initiatives, maturity, but also with the risk of exchange rates.

With that, it is already possible to estimate a revenue of $17.5 billion for Luckin Coffee. If operational leverage continues and margins stabilize, it would be possible to make even bolder estimates, but I preferred to consider only a net margin of 9%. This already means that LKNCY would be trading at a P/E of 6.8x for 2030.

Now think about it: even if at this stage the company can reach 2030, the market still has doubts, and its multiple is only 14x forward. We are talking about an upside of more than 100% in about 5 years. It is a CAGR/total return per year of ~15%.

Therefore, it seems to me to be a much more attractive stock than its peers.

My Fear With Luckin Coffee

Luckin Coffee, as I already mentioned, is Chinese and very dependent on China’s prospects, economy, and so on. For this reason, there is always uncertainty regarding negative surprises and state interference. Personally, this does not concern me that much. The most classic example of this risk was in the education sector, where “suddenly” the government decided that this sector could no longer be profitable. Although it is a (very) controversial decision, it was based on studies and other points related to the Chinese state. The QSR sector, especially in Luckin's format, does not seem to me to be so linked to a greater risk of this level, as it is not so critical to state strategy. Still, even though I don't think the company will eventually be “prohibited” from making a profit, there are still uncertainties such as possible taxation, incentives for other companies to compete with it, some punishment for domination, or something similar.

However, as I mentioned, this is not my main concern. There are two others that, for me, are more important. The first is governance. To say that Luckin's governance history is not the best is an understatement. The truth is that Luckin's history is quite poor in terms of governance. The company inflated its revenue and was fined for it, an event of fraud that led to several people being punished/fired from Luckin.

The good news is that since then, there has been a management overhaul in this regard; the current CEO and COO were not involved in this, and others involved were also removed from the board and replaced. In any case, it is still something that weighs on the case. It is not an event that is so far in the past, and especially given the current high growth, it raises a second flag regarding its sustainability.

What I mean by this is basically a weight on conviction. I don't think coffee is a passing fad; it's an industry that really has very relevant numbers, but believing, for example, in 50,000 stores at such a rapid pace, after Luckin has already “deceived” the market once, makes it difficult to have high conviction.

In other words, even though I don't think this is the base scenario (because if it were, I wouldn't give it a buy rating), there is a world where, in the coming quarters, we discover that this rapid expansion was not so healthy after all, the numbers are revised, and consequently, our upside expectations based on the number of stores and a healthy margin are considerably reduced.

The Bottom Line

Even considering the risks of Luckin Coffee, such as competition, governance, and the risk of being a Chinese company, it is currently a very appealing growth story with absurd upside potential. Therefore, my rating is a cautious buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ASE Technology: AI Is Driving A Great LEAP Forward
data.description:
The more complex and demanding chip design becomes, the more that advanced packaging matters, and that’s directionally positive for ASE Technology (ASX). What’s more, investments in AI are dialing everything up to “11” these days, creating a large and fast-growing opportunity for more lucrative advanced packaging technologies, and ASE Technology is well-placed to exploit this opportunity in 2026 and beyond.

Valuation and expectations are both high, but I believe there’s a credible case for multiple years of double-digit revenue growth and margin leverage as demand for leading-edge advanced packaging and testing (or LEAP) technology grows on the back of AI/high-performance computing demands. Mid-teens revenue growth and 20%-plus EBITDA margin can argue for at least 10%-15% more upside in the near term, but investors should remember that this has historically been a boom-bust sort of industry despite generally attractive backwards-looking growth rates.

Q4 Results Are Likely Derisked, With More Growth on the Way

Given that ASE reports monthly revenue, it gives analysts multiple opportunities to dial in their modeling expectations. To that end, then, we already know that fourth quarter revenue rose almost 10% year over year (and more than 5% sequentially), marking acceleration in the annual growth rate from the third quarter (up 5% yoy and 12% qoq).

The packaging and testing business (Assembly and Testing Services, or “ATM”) saw significant acceleration, with revenue up 24% yoy and 9% in the quarter after 17%/8% growth in the prior quarter. It’s even better in U.S. dollar terms, with growth up 29% yoy and more than 5% qoq. This was stronger than expected and positions the company for a strong 2026, though full details (including utilization rates and margins) will come out next week when the company reports earnings (Feb. 5) and offers guidance.

TSMC Needs ASE, and That’s a Good Situation for ASE

Already the largest independent OSAT (outsourced semiconductor assembly and test) provider, ASE is well-placed to benefit from rapidly growing demand for ever more sophisticated semiconductor packaging.

Advanced packaging provides critical capabilities above and beyond the chip design itself, as offerings like flip-chip, wafer-level fan-out, copper pillar, chip on wafer on substrate (or CoWoS), and fan-out chip-on-substrate (or FoCoS) allow for shorter electrical paths, more efficient heat dissipation, smaller/more compact overall footprints, and different chip types in a single package. As speed, power consumption, heat, and form factor become ever more important, these technologies are critical in driving top performance from GPUs, accelerators, CPUs, networking chips, memory, and so on.

The most advanced of these, at least in large-scale production, is Taiwan Semiconductor’s (TSM) (“TSMC”) CoWoS. TSMC’s branded 2.5D technology, multiple chips are mounted on a silicon interposer, which in turn sits on a packaging substrate—that interposer allows for very dense interconnects between chips, facilitating ultra-high-bandwidth connections between logic and memory, reduced power requirements, and the combination of multiple process nodes.

CoWoS is critical for high-end GPUs like Nvidia’s (NVDA) Blackwell and AMD’s (AMD) MI200/MI300, and some reports suggest that more than 70% of TSMC’s CoWoS capacity is being used for Nvidia chips alone.

With demand also growing from customers like Broadcom (AVGO), Amazon (AMZN), and others, TSMC can’t keep up and is outsourcing some of its CoWoS demand to ASE, setting the stage for meaningful growth in 2026 and beyond, as well as meaningful margin leverage as there’s much less price sensitivity for these highest-end products.

ASE Offers Its Own Proprietary Technologies as Well, and Demand is Growing

CoWoS might be the “sizzle-iest” part of the ASE story in the near term, but ASE doesn’t own or control it, and it’s well worth remembering that ASE has its own packaging technology offers, and AI/HPC ecosystem demand is fueling growth here as well.

ASE’s FoCoS doesn’t compete for the highest-end applications (like leading-edge GPUs), but there are still significant (and growing) addressable opportunities in accelerators, edge AI inference, networking silicon, and mobile processors, where FoCoS is competitive with offerings like TSMC’s SoIC or InFO and Samsung’s I-Cube.

Beyond this are other technologies under the broader umbrella of VIPack (ASE’s advanced packaging offerings), including FoSiP, FOPop, as well as PowerSiP (vertically integrated power that can achieve 50% less routing power loss) and copackaged optics (SiPh).

All told, LEAP likely made up around 12% of ASE’s business in 2025, but this is likely to double in just a couple of years. Not only is ASE set to benefit from GPU/accelerator-driven CoWoS and networking/processor demand for technologies like FoCoS, but edge AI is also a key driver, as edge inference needs the power, heat, and size leverage that advanced packaging offers. ASE is far from the only player in the space, and companies like Amkor (AMKR) are improving their higher-end capabilities, but ASE is well-placed to leverage growing LEAP demand.

As demand accelerates, so too will capacity utilization, and that should not only drive strong operating leverage but also pricing power for these high-end offerings. There is still broader competition in the OSAT space (from companies like Amkor, as well as Chinese OSATs), and utilization has been lackluster due to weaker demand from auto, consumer, and industrial end markets.

AI/HPC is the sizzle here, but the health of the broader semiconductor space is still very relevant. Outsized growth in LEAP coupled with a broader semiconductor recovery should be a powerful one-two punch for ASE over the next two to five years.

The Outlook

Positioning itself to meet this demand comes at a very literal cost for ASE, as the company has significantly ramped up its capex spending to build out capacity for CoWoS and other high-end packaging technologies.

Capex spending has always been consistently higher here than for many other industries (around 11% of revenue over the last decade), but that’s leaping to over 25% in 2025 and will likely stay above 20% for at least a couple of years. Investors interested in exploring the beneficiaries of this capex cycle might want to look at names like Canon (CAJPY), Disco (DCSCY), Tokyo Electron (TOELY), Tokyo Seimitsu (TMUIF), and Veeco (VECO).

I’m expecting mid-teens growth over the next three to five years based on strong demand from leading-edge processors and networking silicon, as well as a broader recovery in overall semiconductor volumes. This demand growth should drive EBITDA to the mid-20%s and operating margins to the mid-to-high teens, with some upside if pricing power really comes into play. I do expect elevated capex to continue for some time, but I do think double-digit free cash flow margins are possible at peak (with longer-term FCF margins likely averaging out in the mid-to-high single digits).

Discounted cash flow does not suggest particularly exciting undervaluation today, but that’s not a surprise given the cyclical nature of the business, the shift in sentiment, and the heavy upfront capex spending. Margin- and growth-driven multiples-based valuation is more promising, though, as the likely margin leverage from LEAP growth supports a 2.25x EV/revenue and 14x EV/EBITDA multiple based on how the market has valued similar fundamentals in the past. That, in turn, offers up about 10% to 15% of near-term upside.

The Bottom Line

ASE isn’t any sort of value-priced buy-and-hold name. This is a very cyclical business, and it has not been that rare to see 30%-plus declines during the downturns. I’d also note that the Street is very much aware of this story, as the shares have roughly doubled in the last six months.

Leading-edge chip demand could well power an extended beat-and-raise cycle, but I would urge investors not to overlook the risks of capacity overbuilding and an eventual slowdown in AI/HPC capex spending. Still, for investors who want a growth/momentum name tied to AI, this seems to be a name with relatively more reasonable expectations embedded into the price.

data.title: Microsoft: The Stars Have Finally Aligned (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

In light of its Q2 FY2026 earnings report, Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) stock is plunging by ~12% to $424 per share on Thursday, 29th January 2026, rendering my past cautious stance on the big tech giant a prescient view:

With MSFT's 5-year expected CAGR return falling well short of TQI's investment hurdle rate of 10% for low volatility, secular growth compounders and the S&P 500's (SPY) long-term average return of ~8-10%, I continue to view Microsoft as a "Neutral/Hold" in the low-$500s post Q1 FY2026 earnings.

From a technical standpoint, Microsoft stock is "Overbought" [weekly RSI >70], and as such, ripe for a corrective pullback.

As a business, I adore Microsoft. However, given MSFT's near-term and long-term risk/reward, I cannot justify underwriting fresh capital allocation into the tech giant at current levels.

Key Takeaway: I rate Microsoft a "Neutral/Hold" in the low-$500s.

Source: Microsoft: Double Beat Not Enough To Justify $4T Market Cap

In this report, we break down Microsoft's latest quarterly results and reassess its long-term risk/reward to reach an informed investment decision.

Analyzing Microsoft's Q2 FY2026 Report

For Q2 FY2026, Microsoft reported a double beat on headline numbers, with revenue coming in at $81.27B [+17% y/y, vs. est. $80.28B] and normalized EPS landing at $4.14 per share [+24% y/y, vs. est. $3.92 per share].

Underneath the surface, healthy growth in Intelligent Cloud [$32.9B, +29% y/y] and Productivity & Business Processes [$34.1B, +16% y/y] segments was offset by weakness in More Personal Computing [$14.3B, -3% y/y] segment.

On the margin front, higher AI infrastructure costs drove ~100 bps y/y contraction in gross margins; however, Microsoft's operating leverage story remained intact, with efficiencies driving operating margins up by ~200 bps y/y, and in turn, pushing operating income +21% y/y to $38.3B.

We are only at the beginning phases of AI diffusion and already Microsoft has built an AI business that is larger than some of our biggest franchises. We are pushing the frontier across our entire AI stack to drive new value for our customers and partners.

- Satya Nadella, chairman and chief executive officer of Microsoft

Microsoft Cloud revenue crossed $50 billion this quarter, reflecting the strong demand for our portfolio of services. We exceeded expectations across revenue, operating income, and earnings per share.

- Amy Hood, executive vice president and chief financial officer of Microsoft

So far, everything looks good; why is Mr. Market reacting so negatively and wiping out ~$400B from Microsoft's market cap?

In my view, the answer is two-pronged. First, Microsoft's AI CAPEX spend jumped up +66% to $37.5B, but Microsoft Azure growth moderated down slightly to 39% y/y in Q2 - missing street whisper numbers of 40-41% growth.

Furthermore, Microsoft's Q3 FY2026 revenue outlook of $80.65 - $81.75B was in line with pre-ER consensus estimates of $81.25B.

Naturally, investors are questioning the ROI on Microsoft's exploding CAPEX spend that is driving a contraction in free cash flow, which fell -9% y/y to $5.9B.

Now, Microsoft did report a +230% y/y jump in commercial bookings for Q2 FY2026, with commercial Remaining Performance Obligation (cRPO) rising to $625B as of 31st December 2025 [up from $392B as of 30th Sep 2025].

However, this step up was driven primarily by deals with OpenAI [$250B [link]] and Anthropic [$30B [link]] - AI startups burning tens of billions of dollars right now. Frankly, OpenAI and Anthropic's ability to pay their massive cloud-computing bills is heavily reliant on capital-market funding. With OpenAI in talks to raise another $60B from Microsoft, Nvidia (NVDA), and Amazon (AMZN), concerns about revenue round-tripping in AI are likely to intensify among investors.

As I see it, Microsoft's aggressive CAPEX spending and failure to accelerate Azure growth (even if it were only due to supply issues) are forcing investors to reevaluate Microsoft's valuation premium.

Microsoft Fair Value And Expected Return

For today's exercise, we're maintaining all prior model assumptions, except for the revenue base, which has been updated to FY2026E revenue of $327B.

Here's my updated valuation for Microsoft:

According to our updated valuation model, Microsoft's fair value estimate is ~$442.6 per share (or ~$3.3T) [up from $439.5 per share (or ~$3.28T) at the previous assessment].

With MSFT stock trading at ~$424 per share, I deem Microsoft to be slightly undervalued.

Now, predicting where a stock will trade in the short term is impossible; however, over the long run, a stock will track its business fundamentals and obey the immutable laws of money. If the interest rates were to return to artificially low levels (i.e., ZIRP), higher equity multiples would be justifiable. However, I work with the assumption that interest rates will eventually track the long-term average of ~5%. Inverting this number, we get a trading multiple of ~20x (P/FCF).

Assuming a base-case exit multiple of 20x P/FCF, I project MSFT stock to rise from ~$424 to ~$693 per share over the next 5 years at a CAGR of 10.3%.

With MSFT's 5-year expected CAGR return moving slightly above TQI's investment hurdle rate of 10% for low volatility, secular growth compounders, and the S&P 500's (SPY) long-term average return of ~8-10%, I now rate Microsoft a modest "Buy" in the low-$400s post Q2 FY2026 earnings.

Key Takeaway: I rate Microsoft a modest "Buy" in the low-$400s.

Thank you for reading, and happy investing! If you have any questions, thoughts, and/or concerns, please share them in the comments section below.

data.title: FuboTV: Synergies With Hulu Yet To Be Priced In
data.description:
CTI initiates coverage on FuboTV Inc. (FUBO), with a Buy Rating and an upside potential of c.50% at the time of writing this article.

Company Overview

FUBO is a live TV streaming service that offers over 200 channels to subscribers in real time as an alternative to traditional cable. This even includes accessing local sports teams by streaming FanDuel Sports Network (FSN) live games or viewing channels through the Fubo app.

The company also provides on-demand programming and digital video recorder (DVR) capabilities, enabling subscribers to record and save content for 90 days on their cloud storage. Additionally, FUBO's unique lookback feature enables subscribers to review up to 72 hours of games/shows that have already aired, ensuring that subscribers remain engaged and keep up-to-date with their routine.

The company also targets sports-focused viewers by providing flexibility for a pure sports package subscription ($55.99/month) that features over 20 sports and broadcast channels. This skinny bundle allows subscribers to further customize sports add-ons based on their interests, such as Sports Plus with NFL RedZone, International Sports Plus, NBA League Pass, and MLB.TV for an additional fee per month.

In October 2025, the company completed its merger with Disney's (DIS) HULU + Live TV segment to create one of the largest virtual Multichannel Video Programming Distributors (vMVPD) in the US with over 6.2 million subscribers. Hulu + Live TV was launched in 2017 as an over-the-top (OTT) paid TV service, offering access to broadcast and cable television channels. Disney will own 70% of the joint venture while FUBO will keep the remaining 30%, despite the combined entity operating entirely under FUBO but their brands sold and marketed separately.

Market Overview

Historically, US sports distribution was optimized via bundled pay-TV economics, where the cost of expensive sports rights was spread across the entire TV population. Cable networks received affiliate fees and advertising revenue that cable operators raked in from households, regardless of whether one consumes sporting content or not.

With the advent of better broadband, the trend of cord-cutting, and the migration of sports rights towards direct-to-consumer access, pay-TV penetration has decreased from c.68% in 2024 to less than half of US households in 2025 as viewers shift from supplementary viewing with a TV box to OTT aggregators via app-based and connected-TV streaming platforms. Hence, streaming platforms not only emerged as an alternative delivery channel but also as a strategic lever for the acquisition of subscribers and monetization via advertisements.

From a total of $84.8 billion in 2025, the global sports streaming platform is predicted to increase at a CAGR of 6.24% for around a decade. The adoption of OTT sports streaming is well supported by the ease of cross-border fandom, globalization of sporting leagues, and the lack of innovation of traditional pay-TV bundles in many markets.

Competitive Landscape

Over the years, the OTT sports streaming landscape has evolved into a highly competitive, segmented environment that is defined by different approaches to content aggregation, viewership retention, and monetization.

vMVPDs (i.e., YouTube TV and FUBO) position themselves as digital versions of traditional pay-TV bundles, emphasizing comprehensive coverage of channels and live sports with the cloud DVR functionality.

In contrast, network-owned streaming platforms (i.e., ESPN+, Peacock, Paramount+) deploy sports content selectively, utilizing exclusive rights to differentiate from peers. This helps to reduce churn and support monetization via advertisements.

Lastly, technology platforms (i.e., Amazon Prime Video, Apple TV+) are an up-and-coming group that approaches sporting events completely differently from the rest. They acquire high-profile events and leverage live sports to reinforce the stickiness of their ecosystem. At the same time, this helps them to scale advertising inventory, enhancing cross-platform monetization.

Business Overview

FUBO generates revenue primarily through subscriptions (93% of TTM total revenue) and the remainder through advertisements, distribution fees, commissions, and carriage fees via channel distribution platforms. The company markets three main subscriptions: Pro, Elite, and Latino, while pocketing fees for add-on services such as cloud DVR, streaming devices, channel packages, and regional sports network fees.

Packages Price/month Channels Streams
Pro $84.99 219 10
Elite $94.99 291 10
Latino $14.99 50 2

Growth Drivers

FUBO is focused on growing its subscriber base by pricing its subscription plans holistically. FUBO's sports skinny bundle provides high-value access to top sports content at a lower price while expanding their pay-per-view initiatives across live events. These initiatives not only better cater to the company's target audience but also increase its focus on premium add-ons that contribute to subscription revenue.

On average, subscribers in the US are reported to spend an average of $125 per month on their cable/satellite TV subscription, and c.43% of Gen Z and millennials surveyed indicated their willingness to pay to access live sports for subscription video on demand (SVOD). Hence, we believe that FUBO possesses a strong bargaining power to hike subscription prices gradually [while keeping marketing and other variable costs low...].

Enhancement of the platform by integrating features (i.e., Multiview, Game highlights, Game alert push notifications, and Catch up to live) that provide subscribers personalized capabilities and build habits. This increases the time subscribers are engaged on FUBO's platform and reduces churn. We believe that reducing churn is key for FUBO's business model, as the costs of acquiring customers (CAC) are high and the costs of reacquiring them are even higher.

Investment Thesis

Realization of Synergies With Disney

After completing the recent merger with DIS, we believe that FUBO will benefit from a more flexible programming package. Besides, Disney has a proven track record of realizing synergies with Hulu in 2019 as part of its acquisition of 21st Century Fox and subsequent full control in June 2025.

By end-2019, the price of HULU + Live TV has increased from $44.99 to $54.99, before increasing to $64.99 by the end of 2020. In the same period, subscribers continued to rise from 2.7 million to 4.1 million, outlining the strength of its offerings as a newly combined entity.

We expect FUBO to gradually possess similar bargaining powers with regards to pricing its offerings.

Increase in Strategic Distribution Partnerships

Besides pricing power, FUBO stands to gain more distribution partnerships as the second largest vMVPD in the US after this merger. Currently, FUBO's distribution partnerships are limited to DAZN, Newsmax (NMAX), over-the-air TV stations, and free ad-supported streaming TV ('FAST') networks. We expect FUBO to introduce more partnerships with OTT sports streaming services, internet service providers (ISPs), and technology platforms. These partnerships extend reach into subscriber pools with lower marginal acquisition costs and exhibit a more inelastic change in demand when prices of offerings increase.

Regarding FUBO's dispute with NBCUniversal's Peacock, we view it as a positive signal for its strategic pivot away from the traditional cable-style bundles towards flexible, cost-effective skinny bundles. Hence, we expect less focus on partnerships with cable operators moving forward.

Elevated Short Volume is Susceptible to Short Squeeze

FUBO's YTD short interest has risen significantly to 18.1% as of the date of writing this article.

For the past 30 days, FUBO's average short volume had been c.39% (highlighted in red). This indicates a short-term bearish sentiment, but upon examining its TTM short volume data, we find this bearish sentiment extends across a longer historical time horizon.

We believe that the market had not truly priced in the potential synergies from the merger and expect a significant boost in share prices when the company releases new partnerships.

Financials

Income Statement

Despite subscribers paying fixed subscription fees in advance on a monthly basis, FUBO recognizes subscription revenue ratably over the lifetime of a subscription. For advertising revenue, it consists of fees charged to advertisers that display ads within streamed content, primarily based on the number of impressions to be delivered. Fubo recognizes advertising revenue when advertisements have been displayed.

Both streams of revenue are primarily driven by the number of subscribers on FUBO's platform and viewership of content on the platform.

Balance Sheet

As of 3Q25, FUBO had cash and cash equivalents of $280.3M that is primarily invested in money market funds. Although its current ratio of 0.7x may reveal concerns with the company's ability to operate in the short term, FUBO exercised its option and borrowed $145M from an affiliate of DIS via a term loan to service notes maturing in 2026. This leaves FUBO to service notes maturing in 2029, where existing holders elect not to tender their notes for repurchase or convert their debt into equity.

As a result, we find that FUBO's debt-to-equity ratio of 96% has room for improvement, given that the current combined entity's ratio is relatively lower than historicals and its asset-light business model.

Unlike its peers, FUBO does not produce its own on-demand programming for streaming and is heavily dependent on licensing content from third-party providers. Besides, FUBO had already built out a significant portion of its proprietary video infrastructure to manage, transcode, and deliver live video, utilizing third parties to help scale and meet demand.

Hence, we believe that FUBO is unlikely to incur large amounts of capex moving forward and would not need to issue debt to finance large, long-term assets.

We also note that a significant portion of the company's assets are recorded as goodwill and intangible assets. As of the end of FY24, the weighted average remaining life of its intangible assets is 3.7 years and will be amortized on a straight-line basis. We noted no significant changes in goodwill and will be expecting the same moving forward.

Cash Flow

FUBO relies a lot on its cash flow for content licensing fees and operating expenses. The company is expected to generate a positive change in net working capital and a positive cash flow from operations for FY25 full year, despite experiencing a historical trend of increasing accounts receivable and decreasing prepaid expenses that caused days sales outstanding and days payable outstanding to increase and decrease, respectively.

We note that historical stock-based compensation (SBC) significantly impacts cash flows from operations (CFO). With the exception of 3 million shares available for future issuance under the 2025 Employment Inducement Incentive Plan, there are currently no plans for further issuances under FUBO's 2022, 2023, and 2024 plans for significant near-term dilution. As of 3Q25, there are 343 million shares of common stock issued by FUBO.

1Q26 Financials Expectation

In the upcoming 1Q26 earnings call, we are looking out for the management's guidance on average revenue per user for the upcoming fiscal year post-merger. For FY24 full year, FUBO reported $85.97 average revenue per user (ARPU), and we expect this to tick up slightly from ongoing synergies with DIS.

For 3Q25, the company reported 1.63 million paid subscribers, down from 1.68 million for FY24 full year. We expect the management to guide a slower churn in paid subscribers for the upcoming fiscal year as paid subscribers hold steady for the quarter.

We are also looking out for the management's guidance on marketing spend, as it is key in understanding synergies in the partnership between FUBO and DIS moving forward.

Valuation

We rate FUBO as a Buy, with a price target of $3.56. We applied an EV/Revenue of 0.9x to our FY25 full-year expected revenue of $1.49 billion to arrive at our current enterprise and equity value. Historically, FUBO's multiple fluctuated between 0.4x - 0.9x, and we believe that FUBO will command a higher premium as the company marks its second consecutive quarter of positive adjusted EBITDA in 3Q25. Our assumptions follow a 3-year (FY25-FY27) forecast and the details below.

We hold Pro, Elite, and Latino subscription prices constant and a decrease in ARPU for FY25, given a slowing churn in the number of paying subscribers. For FY26, we continue to model a churn in the number of paying subscribers before stabilizing heading into FY27. For advertising revenue, we grow it in line with the trend of paying subscribers stated above.

Final Thoughts

After its merger with DIS's Hulu + Live TV, FUBO's business model remains fundamentally unchanged. We expect new partnerships to act as positive catalysts for FUBO's share price for investors over the long term, and its high short interest to deliver short-term gains for traders.

data.title: Celestica Is Now A De-Risked AI Infrastructure Compounder
data.description:
Celestica Inc. (CLS) has delivered a strong Q4 beat and raise, underscoring continued durability to its competitive advantage in serving surging AI infrastructure demand. The latest results also directly address recent concerns about key partner Google’s (GOOG / GOOGL) potential diversification of its TPU supply chain to rivals. Specifically, management reaffirmed Celestica’s role as Google’s “preferred manufacturing partner” for its data center hardware, including the TPU systems, and disclosed the company’s long-term growth investment plans to support the ongoing partnership.

Yet the stock fell by as much as 8.5% in pre-market trading Thursday (January 29) before opening about 3.7% lower ($332.04) from the prior day’s close ($344.70) following Celestica’s Q4 earnings release. At the time of writing, the stock’s intraday losses have exceeded 15%. The correction imitates recent post-earnings weakness observed across key participants in the broader AI value chain that have reported better-than-expected results, including Microsoft (MSFT).

In the following analysis, I dive into why Celestica’s valuation has retracted despite the robust beat and raise. Then, I explain further why this could represent a compelling opportunity, as the latest pullback meaningfully de-risks the stock while Celestica continues to consistently execute against its growth roadmap.

Stock Adjustment is a Direct Result of Compressed ROI Expectations

Celestica has released its Q4 earnings results yesterday after market close (January 28), with results reflecting a robust double beat against previously raised expectations for both the quarter and full year 2025.

The company reported Q4 revenue of 44% y/y to $3.7 billion, outperforming the upper range of previously raised guidance by more than $80 million and the average consensus estimate by $160 million. This has resulted in full-year 2025 revenue growth of 28% y/y to $12.4 billion, outpacing the company’s previously raised outlook by $191 million. Its earnings metrics also outperformed, with a Q4 adjusted operating margin of 7.7% outperforming the previously guided 7.6%, driving accretion towards the full year 2025 adjusted operating margin towards 7.5%. Q4 adjusted EPS grew by more than 70% y/y and 20% q/q to $1.89, resulting in full year 2025 adjusted EPS of $6.05, up 56% y/y.

This accordingly underscores continued operating leverage improvements, alongside scaling unit economic benefits as its core AI-driven Connectivity & Cloud Solutions (“CCS”) segment sales ramp.

The robust Q4 report has also underpinned a raised full-year 2026 outlook. Celestica now expects current year revenue of $17 billion and adjusted EPS of $8.75 for the current year. This compares to the previous guidance for full year 2026 revenue of $16 billion and adjusted EPS of $8.20. To support the incremental growth opportunities ahead, management has guided $1 billion in current year capex, which represents multi-fold growth from about $200 million deployed through full year 2025. This aligns with Celestica’s ongoing capacity expansion plans across its manufacturing and design facilities in Texas, as well as overseas in Southeast Asia, Japan, and Mexico to support the increasing mix of higher-value opportunities across its AI/ML and Hardware Platform Solutions (“HPS”) programs.

However, the full year 2026 guidance for free cash flow of $500 million remains unchanged from previous expectations. This likely implies ROI compression from expectations set under the previous guidance, despite the anticipated uplift to Celestica’s full-year growth and earnings outlook. The dynamics potentially overshadowed the company’s otherwise strong Q4 print and drove the stock’s post-earnings correction.

This would be consistent with recent investor sentiment in assessing the performance of key participants across the broader AI value chain:

  • Microsoft: Despite both the fiscal Q1 and Q2 earnings outperformance, the Microsoft stock has continued to sell off. This reflects continued market adjustments to its valuation outlook, as repeatedly higher-than-expected AI capex lacks a corresponding uplift in its growth and earnings profile, suggesting ROI compression.
  • Meta Platforms (META): The stock fell following its Q3 outperformance due to similar ROI uncertainties observed from Microsoft. Specifically, the company’s better-than-expected earnings results in Q3 were overshadowed by a raised capex outlook with limited corresponding uplift to its growth and earnings trajectory. This effectively raised concerns that impending AI returns would be more expensive than expected, resulting in a correction to the stock’s valuation premium. Yet this trend’s been reversed following Meta’s robust Q4 beat and better-than-expected forward guidance, reinforcing visibility into the company’s AI returns amid rising capex spend.
  • Google and Amazon (AMZN): Both stocks have been benefitting from strong post-Q3 earnings gains, despite having raised capex expectations. This is largely due to their delivery of matching uplifts in their respective growth prospects, which mitigates against ROI compression risks and preserves FCF stability critical to their valuations.

Key Tailwinds Ahead

Yet despite risks of ROI compression as Celestica continues to position itself to better capture pent-up end-market demand, its underlying business fundamentals remain supportive of strong growth visibility. This underscores sustained FCF accretion, which is critical for driving further upside potential from the stock’s current levels.

High-Growth Product Roadmap

Celestica continues to execute consistently against its high-growth roadmap. This is evidenced in sustained acceleration in CCS Communications end-market sales, reflecting the company’s continued competitive advantage in serving high-value demand for its AI-focused HPS program.

Specifically, Celestica delivered about $1.4 billion in Q4 HPS revenue, up 72% y/y. The accompanying earnings expansion also underscores a sustained mix shift towards the higher value HPS business, which is already margin accretive to overall CCS Communications sales.

Looking ahead, the current HPS growth and earnings trajectory reinforces ROI visibility for Celestica’s ongoing capacity expansion and R&D investment efforts.

It’s likely HPS’s mix of Celestica’s total revenue will remain on the rise, as its newest 1.6T switch optimized for AI data center applications starts to ramp alongside its 800G predecessor this year. Recall that management had secured two hyperscaler customers for the 1.6T switch by Q4 2024, which were expected to start converting into revenue in 2026:

First, on the networking front, we have been awarded our second 1.6T switching program with another large Hyperscaler customer, which will begin ramping in 2026. This HPS program award includes supporting our customer with the design and production of a fully AI optimized networking rack, which will leverage our advanced system level cooling technology.

Source: Celestica Q4 2024 Earnings Call Transcript.

Long-term demand visibility for the new program is further reinforced by the company’s recent addition of a third hyperscaler customer for the new 1.6T program, which is expected to start ramping in 2027:

Celestica has been awarded the program for the design and manufacturing of the 1.6 terabyte switching platform with a third hyperscaler customer. The HPS program is expected to begin ramping production in 2027.

Source: Celestica Q4 2025 Earnings Press Release.

And Celestica remains well positioned to execute on converting these program wins into sustained HPS growth and earnings accretion. Specifically, following the introduction of its newest 1.6T switch in October, Celestica’s been rapidly incorporating the technology into next-generation rack-scale system designs, accelerating the end market’s time-to-value. Not only is this key to addressing rack-scale requirements outlined for the related contract win disclosed in late 2024, but this rapid execution has also played a critical role in capturing new program awards.

This is corroborated by Celestica’s efforts in becoming the first in the world to incorporate the 1.6T switch with next-generation hardware—potentially including the latest generation of Nvidia (NVDA) Blackwell Ultra GPUs, which require liquid cooling due to power intensity, and Broadcom’s (AVGO) latest Tomahawk 6 switch:

Next, I want to briefly detail a case study that will help bring to life our competitive differentiation enabled by our core success drivers. In this instance, a hyperscaler customer approached us to collaborate on the design of our first of its kind rack-scale liquid cool 1.6T networking solution needed in order to accommodate the increased density and power requirements of the latest generation AI networking platforms…The customer required an accelerated road map to allow the solution to be early to market, leveraging Broadcom's Tomahawk 6 SC silicon, making speed to market a key consideration…They also valued our advanced manufacturing capabilities, specifically our ability to operationalize highly complex production lines for liquid cooled racks at scale and to do this faster and more seamlessly than other potential partners. After receiving initial Tomahawk 6 samples earlier this year, we quickly stood up an operational prototype for the 1.6T switch and believe we were the first team anywhere to have done so.

Source: Celestica Q3 2025 Earnings Call Transcript.

These dynamics remain key reinforcements to visibility on sustained returns from its expanding footprint that’ll be gradually coming online through 2028—including dedicated HPS program expansion across Southeast Asia and Japan overseas, as well as Richardson in the U.S. They’ll also potentially deem management’s latest guidance conservative, as the favorable mix shift towards margin-accretive HPS program sales continues to accelerate.

Google TPU Program Expansion

Impending prospects of better-than-expected upside and incremental share gains are further reinforced by management’s recent reaffirmation that Celestica remains Google’s preferred manufacturing partner for its TPU systems, as well as other existing and next-generation data center hardware. This directly addresses recent concerns that Google may be diversifying some of its TPU server volumes to other suppliers, including Inventec.

More importantly, management has also highlighted that part of Celestica’s U.S. expansion efforts, which accounts for part of its accelerating capex trajectory, will be dedicated towards supporting Google’s next-generation data center hardware builds. Based on the anticipated completion timeline for related expansion efforts in 2027, it implies the incremental volumes will start ramping later that year or early 2028, further increasing visibility into Celestica’s sustained growth momentum.

The latest development will also complement existing expansion efforts overseas, particularly in Southeast Asia, as well, which management disclosed will be dedicated to supporting “Google’s TPU platform and leading-edge networking technologies.”

As discussed in my recent coverage on Google, its TPU roadmap is expected to emerge as a key growth driver for the cloud unit. This is largely due to the chip’s competitive performance and token unit economics, which align well with the increasing shift in AI compute towards high-volume inference workloads where scalability considerations come first. And Celestica’s upcoming capacity expansion plans to accommodate this momentum are expected to reinforce durability to its growth outlook.

More importantly, the expansion also provides incremental validation to Celestica’s performance, quality, and reliability—aligning directly with one of its three key success pillars:

Third, we are steadfastly focused on maintaining best-in-class operational execution. Our global footprint, combined with the rigorous processes of the Celestica Operating System, ensures we can manufacture and deliver the highest complexity products without compromising quality and reliability.

Source: Celestica Q3 2025 Earnings Call Transcript.

Admittedly, Google’s diversification of its TPU server manufacturing capacity is inevitable in order to protect its interests and manage supply chain risks. However, with volume allocations primarily “[dependent] on performance and quality,” management’s recent confirmation that Celestica remains Google’s preferred TPU server manufacturer effectively reinforces its competitive advantage. This is also expected to underpin sustained expansion in Celestica’s demand pipeline, particularly for its ramping HPS roadmap optimized for AI data center applications.

Fundamental Considerations

Taking into consideration Celestica’s latest Q4 results and forward outlook, I expect the company to deliver full-year 2026 revenue growth of 50% y/y towards $18.6 billion. The assumption reflects an increasing ramp for its 1.6T switch program alongside continued momentum for the 800G switches, which remain highly accretive to its earnings. In the longer term, it’s likely Celestica remains well-positioned to maintain strong double-digit percentage growth over the next four years, with revenue to expand at a 27% CAGR over the same period. This will be primarily driven by increasing capacity for its HPS roadmap, which remains in high-demand amid secular AI transformation dynamics, reinforced by long-term hyperscaler customer programs secured through at least 2027.

The sustained mix shift towards higher-margin HPS sales is expected to reinforce operating leverage gains as well, supporting a durable trajectory of continued earnings accretion. This remains key for sustaining FCF expansion to support Celestica’s accelerated growth capex trajectory while underpinning prospects of further ROI expansion critical for its valuation outlook.

Price Considerations

Taken together, I set my base case price target for Celestica at $370 per share. This represents upside potential of 11% from its current price of $332.04 per share (January 29 open).

The price target is derived under the discounted cash flow (“DCF”) valuation method, which considers projections in line with the above base case fundamental forecast. It considers a terminal value of $48.4 billion determined by the application of a 1.5% perpetual growth rate on projected 2030 EBITDA. The perpetual growth rate assumption is consistent with the anticipated pace of long-term economic expansion across Celestica’s core markets and reflects the rate at which its steady-state cash flows—proxied by 2030 EBITDA—can sustainably expand at the mature business cycle. While the AI transformation is likely to extend beyond 2030, I expect Celestica’s FCF accretion to slow by then, as new infrastructure builds are completed and the pace of incremental deployments starts to normalize.

Total cash flow projections, including the terminal value, are discounted at a WACC of 9.4%, which is in line with Celestica’s capital structure and risk profile.

Risks to Consider

Despite Celestica’s ongoing execution consistency and improving growth visibility reinforced by customer programs locked in through at least 2027, looming ROI compression risks discussed earlier could remain an overhang on the stock. This accordingly raises Celestica’s urgency to ramp its current 800G and new 1.6T switch programs, specifically, given their higher margins.

The near-term ROI overhang is further complicated by the complexity of Celestica’s increasing capacity expansion plans. These efforts, alongside the ongoing ramp of new products, could introduce incremental execution costs in the near term. Any incremental earnings pressure, even if temporary, could result in misalignment with its intensifying capex plans and risk tempering investors’ confidence.

Final Thoughts

Based on the foregoing analysis, it’s evident that Celestica’s growth and earnings accretion trajectory remains durable, reinforced by increased visibility into large hyperscaler customer programs secured through at least 2027 for its high-margin HPS product roadmap. This accordingly aligns with the company’s accelerating growth investment efforts, with increased production capacity underscoring further growth in the long run. The increasing mix shift towards higher-margin HPS programs focused on addressing pent-up demand from AI data center applications also bolsters prospects of further earnings and FCF accretion.

While signals of potential near-term ROI compression have likely weighed on investors’ sentiment, Celestica’s heightened capex outlook likely reflects the front-loading of investments to better capture long-term AI infrastructure opportunities instead. And these opportunities likely remain strong based on its disclosed program wins to date. Taken together, the stock’s latest post-earnings pullback has accordingly offered a better de-risked opportunity to partake in further upside potential, sustained by Celestica’s improving long-term earnings and FCF profile.

data.title: Valero Energy: Solid Q4 Is Reflected In Valuation
data.description:
Shares of Valero Energy (VLO) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining 35% as the refining macro environment is considerably better than 12 months ago. Ongoing capital returns have also supported shares. The company reported another strong quarter on Thursday morning, sending shares up another 2%. I last covered Valero in December when I downgraded shares to a “Hold,” but with the stock gaining another 7% since then, I should have kept them a “Buy,” though they did first approach my $160 buy target before rebounding.

Regime change actions in Venezuela (which I did not foresee) have been a key driver of outperformance, as that may increase VLO’s access to low-cost heavy crude oil. With updated financials and new macro developments, now is a good time to revisit Valero.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Valero earned $3.82 per share, blowing past estimates by $0.55. Revenue fell by 1% to $30 billion, though this is a less relevant metric for refiners. Gasoline prices significantly impact revenue, but refineries make money on the spread between gasoline/diesel and crude oil rather than on their absolute price, which is why a focus on crack spreads and refining margins is more relevant than revenue and price levels.

Crack spread improvement drives better results

Its refining segment earned $1.7 billion in Q4, nearly quadrupling the $441 million earned last year. Throughput was strong at 3.1mbd, which was up about 4% from last year. Refining margins per barrel were up to $13.61 from $8.44 last year, given the more favorable crack spread environment. Partially offsetting this, operating expenses were up about 8% to $5.03. I do expect operating cost pressures to persist this year. Refining is an energy-intensive process, and as you can see below, electricity prices have surged, in part due to increased demand from data centers. That pressure is likely to persist at least through 2026.

Ultimately though, crack spreads (the differential between crude oil input costs and product output prices) are what drive the business. Spreads surged in the first half of Q4 past $30, then plunged back towards $20, and have more recently recovered. While crack spreads are volatile, the level of volatility we have seen over the past four months has been unusual. Still, as you can see over Q4, spreads were safely above levels seen last year, which is why refining profits were up so substantially.

The question for investors is what the future environment will look like. I have written previously that I view $25 as a “normal” long-term crack spread. I downgraded VLO to “Hold” late last year in part because spreads had gotten beyond this level. Crack spreads are driven by the relative supply/demand of crude vs. refined products. Over the past few months, we have seen gasoline inventories surge from very low levels to very high levels (even considering the seasonal buildup during Q4). As gasoline inventories surged, this put pressure on crack spreads.

Frankly, the moves in crack spreads in both directions were a bit exaggerated, and spreads are now at $25 again, a healthy level consistent with my long-term view. Given my view that the United States is unlikely to have a recession this year, I expect stable gasoline demand this year, which should limit inventory builds. Plus, refineries are doing more seasonal maintenance in Q1, which temporarily reduces supply. Finally, the incremental supply from Venezuela is likely playing a role in the rebound we are seeing and has certainly altered sentiment in the sector.

Venezuela is an incremental positive

Earlier this month, the Trump Administration deposed Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro, leading to regime change there. Venezuelan oil production has fallen significantly over the past decade given U.S. sanctions and mismanagement. Friendlier relations could lead to incremental investment and more production, reducing crude oil prices and widening crack spreads. This is especially positive for U.S. refiners, which are built to process the cheaper, heavy crude that Venezuela produces. Now, there is still a massive amount of uncertainty as to what will happen here, and I expect upstream producers will be cautious before investing significant sums.

As such, most of the potential benefits of higher production are unlikely to be realized before 2028, and there is a risk that Venezuelan production never rises much. Still, there are near-term benefits as friendlier relations have shifted oil flows, with Venezuela selling more to the United States. Indeed, Valero purchased one of the first Venezuelan cargos at a sizable discount to benchmark prices. All else equal, actions in Venezuela should lift VLO’s refining margin, but the impact is unlikely to be more than $0.50/barrel in my view. This is an incremental, not a game-changing positive.

I would also note that Valero is in the process of closing its Benicia refinery in California, though it has delayed the closure and will continue to produce gasoline through April after working with the governor. Unique regulations isolate the California gasoline market, and they also make it harder to profitably run a refinery in the state. I have been assuming a total closure of the facility, and I do not view this extension as material to investors.

Overall, I expect the crack spread environment to average about $25 in 2026. That said, in the near term, I see risks skewed to the downside, given the elevated level of gasoline inventories currently. Still for the full year, this will be an improvement over average 2025 levels and could provide an incremental ~$2 billion of free cash flow and ~$6 in EPS to the ~$10.61 reported in full year 2025. This also assumes a normal hurricane season, which can impact Gulf Coast throughput, a negative for VLO, though that often also leads to temporarily wider crack spreads.

Its renewable diesel unit earned $92 million, down from $170 million last year, as increased supply has weighed on margins. On the other hand, ethanol operating income was up $97 million to $117 million. Combined, these smaller, “green” units contributed similar earnings as a year ago. Given the pressures farmers are facing from the trade war, President Trump has suggested his support for higher ethanol standards year-end to make up for lost exported demand. This would be a positive for Valero, likely increasing margins on its production. Still, I expect these units to have flattish results in 2026 relative to 2025, and I view refining as the core of Valero’s earnings power and valuation.

The balance sheet enables capital returns

The company maintains an excellent balance sheet with $4.7 billion of cash and $8.3 billion of debt. That leaves the company with less than 1x net leverage, and I expect VLO to use all free cash flow for buybacks and dividends. Indeed, during Q4, VLO returned $1.4 billion to shareholders, bringing full-year capital returns to $4 billion. Thanks to buybacks, its share count was down about 4% from last year. I expect a somewhat faster pace of repurchases in 2026. Earlier this month, Valero also increased its dividend by 6%, and shares now offer a 2.6% dividend yield.

Back in December, I saw the company having $17 of run-rate capital return capacity, and this remains my view. I expect a ~5% share count reduction this year, which leaves the company with about a 7.6% capital return yield. This is certainly not unattractive, though the refining sector has a generally cheap valuation. While a company like Marathon Petroleum (MPC) has less Venezuela benefit, I expect it to have a capital return above 11% this year.

Shares are a suitable "Hold"

With the lack of long-term capacity growth, I view refineries as the long-term “bottleneck” in the petroleum supply chain, a positive for long-term margins, and as such, I am positively predisposed towards the sector. However, long-term obsolescence concerns (given ongoing electrification and fuel efficiency gains) along with their cyclicality are likely to structurally limit multiple expansion.

Within this construct and relative to refining peers, I view Valero as fairly valued, especially as I am cautious on the ultimate magnitude of Venezuela benefits. Given capital returns, I view VLO as a suitable “Hold” in portfolios, but with gasoline inventories elevated and a premium multiple vs. MPC (my top pick in the sector), I see better relative opportunities and would want to see shares pull back into the mid-$170s before adding. For short-term traders, I am cautious on crack spreads over the next 1-2 months given the elevated level of inventories, and a turn lower could put some downside pressure on shares, which is why I believe a better entry point may appear. For longer-term investors, I do not view 1-2 month movements in crack spreads as a reason to buy/sell, and with its capital return capacity, VLO is a suitable hold over a 12-month time horizon.

data.title: Silver Is In The Middle Of Repricing, Not The Peak Of Repricing
data.description:
There have been a lot of warning bells against silver since the beginning of this year. The reweighing argument took me out of a $66 strike call option on SLV (SLV). I sold it at a profit, but in hindsight, that was liquidated too early. I also hold physical silver, and more recently, I have been thinking of building a larger position in the metal. With some time to kill on a long flight, I thought I’d evaluate whether that instinct was driven by something more durable in the setup.

Initially I went back to first principles, which in this exercise was to find things that drive silver prices.

Why Supply/Demand Deficits Are Not a Good Indicator for Price Movements?

In 2024, the Silver Institute published a report on the factors that determine the price of silver. Intuitively, it is easy to link industrial supply and demand as a key source of price movement. While this forms the dominant narrative, the correlation statistics from the report suggest price is driven mainly by investment and bullion setups.

A cursory look at the decade-long deficit/surplus trajectory actually supports this view. In the 1990s, prices remained depressed in the $4-$6/oz range despite persistent supply deficits. In the 2009-2011 period, prices surged despite surpluses. And more recently, supply deficits since the 2020s produced a price surge only in 2025. The price movements during these periods are better explained by investor behavior, as they tend to mop up supply during surpluses and sell holdings during deficits.

Now that I know that investment demand is the most important price mover, I thought I’d look at current configurations and find a range for the price of silver.

First Method: Gold-to-Silver Reversion Model

The gold-to-silver ratio (or GSR) is a relative valuation tool preferred even by institutional desks. Apart from the strong correlation seen between the metals, it negates the effect of investor interest in safe haven assets. As an example, both silver and gold climbed up as risk-off sentiments increased during the Davos summit. The GSR fell but not as dramatically as the increase in gold and silver prices.

Over the last year, the ratio has been on a dramatic descent from 100x in March 2025 to around 47x now. So, the easy money in the reversion trade has already been captured. To find the lower end of the range, I anchored silver prices to expected targets on gold and used the 50x multiple from the 80/50 heuristic. This comes out to around $120 if you consider that gold is within reach of its $6,000 target.

To find the higher end of the range, I applied the historical lows in the ratio to this gold price anchor. Obviously, the 1980s ratio would result in an outlandish target. But considering that even the 2011 ratio of 32x results in a $120 - $187.50 range, the setup for silver is still skewed to the upside.

GSR Ratio Gold anchor Silver target
1980 15 $6,000 $400.00
2011 32 $6,000 $187.50
2026 50 $6,000 $120.00

The Davos summit was still ongoing when I first came up with this range. At this point, I thought I had enough to go on and make a decision and also became a bit impatient reading a research paper for the second method. So I bought a call option on SLV again with a strike price of $89. I sold this at nearly 3.7 times my cost on the 26th of January. It is important to note that for continued exposure, the expiry on the option forces a sell and a buyback at an expiry with a later date. While it amplifies returns, it is also a very risky way to gain exposure to SLV. Regardless, I bought a $118 call the next day and again sold it at a ~55% profit.

Second Method: M2 Money Supply Catch-up Trade

There is a cointegration study that shows a statistically significant relationship between M2 money supply and gold prices over 50-year periods. The correlation is very low in the short term. The Silver Institute report mentioned earlier also cites silver’s correlation with gold being high only in the long term. These dual correlations (Gold–M2 and Silver–Gold) could mean silver acts like a coiled spring, ignoring money supply for years before violently repricing in a short window. This looks a lot like what is happening right now.

For this calculation, we must find the expansion factor or the ratio of the current money supply to the money supply at previous peaks. The question we are trying to answer is, “What is $50 in silver from 1980 worth in today’s dollars, after adjusting for money supply?"

As seen above, we get a range of $123 to $697. The physical shortage we saw in October 2025 is similar to the 1980s peak when the Hunt brothers tried to corner the market. However, considering that $123 is still at the lower end of the range, I think the warnings on the metal seem misplaced at this point in time. I am therefore maintaining a “buy” rating on silver.

Caveats and Conclusion

In parabolic moves like the one silver is making, exchanges often raise margin requirements. This can force traders to sell positions regardless of conviction. This can be very violent with 20%-30% shakeouts even if the long-term trend remains intact. I'd therefore caution investors to look into their risk profiles while buying exposure to this metal.

Against plenty of warning bells, I think the silver market is not just in a bull market but in a structural repricing event. I think investors should look at the current breakout as a secular shift with the $100 becoming a floor rather than a ceiling in the coming year. I intend to hold and add physical silver to ride volatility while using options to capture the likely explosive upside of M2 reversion.

data.title: The Humanoids Have Arrived! At CES 2026
data.description:
By now, the headline statistics have been well publicized. Goldman Sachs (GS) estimates annual humanoid robot shipments could reach one million units by the early to mid-2030s.1 Morgan Stanley (MS) goes further, projecting more than one billion humanoids deployed globally by 2050.2 The numbers are eye-popping - but at this year’s Consumer Electronics Show (CES) in Las Vegas, I saw something more important than forecasts. I saw the humanoid future moving out of research labs and into the real world.

CES is known to be a proving ground for technologies on the verge of mass adoption.3Whether it was the VCR in the 1970s, the CD player in the early 1980s, DVDs in the 1990s, or smart TVs and mobile computing in the 2000s, the show has a long track record of spotlighting innovations just before they enter daily life. This year, from what I saw, I believe humanoid robots fit that pattern.

Walking the halls of CES, I encountered dozens of new humanoid robot brands - alongside component manufacturers, data-collection platforms, operating systems, and embodied-intelligence software providers. One thing quickly became obvious: the epicenter of this ecosystem is China. As seen in the chart below, many humanoid exhibitors either came directly from China or were founded by Chinese engineers now operating in Silicon Valley or elsewhere. Mandarin was spoken as frequently as English. I believe this may be a sign of an improving geopolitical atmosphere, as management teams were eyeing the US market and were enthusiastic about their future business prospects here.

It was encouraging to see how closely the conference aligned with the investment framework of our KraneShares Global Humanoid and Embodied Intelligence ETF (KOID). KOID is designed to capture the broad humanoid value chain: the components, the brains, and the integrators building real-world humanoid systems across the U.S., China, Europe, and broader Asia. CES confirmed that this ecosystem is not theoretical - it is already forming at scale.

With that opportunity comes risk.
